items.title: Incubation
items.contentHtml:
Incubation period

  • The incubation period of chickenpox is 14-16 days (range 10-21 days).
  • It is the time from exposure to the varicella-zoster virus until the appearance of the rash.
  • Transmissibility begins 1-2 days before the rash, during the prodrome. febrile.
  • In immunocompromised patients, the period can last up to 28 days after exposure.
  • Knowing this interval is key to calculating the window for post-exposure prophylaxis with specific immunoglobulin (up to 96 hours).

items.foco: Centripetal rash with macules, papules, vesicles and crusts
items.title: Characteristic rash
items.contentHtml:
Centripetal rash

  • The rash is centripetal: it predominates on the trunk and face, with less involvement on the extremities.
  • It evolves in 24-48 hours from macules (1-3 mm) to papules, then vesicles (with clear content, “dewdrop”) and finally scabs.
  • The lesions appear in successive waves, so different stages coexist in the same body region (evolutionary polymorphism).
  • The vesicles are umbilicate and break easily, forming scabs that fall off in 7-10 days without leaving a scar if there is no superinfection.
  • Differential diagnosis: impetigo blistering (meliceric scabs, perioral distribution), disseminated herpes simplex (grouped lesions, recurrences), enterovirus (vesicular rash on palms and soles).

items.foco: Transmission by respiratory droplets and direct contact
items.title: Transmission
items.contentHtml:
Transmission

  • Transmission occurs by respiratory droplets (coughing, sneezing) and by direct contact with the fluid from the vesicles.
  • There can also be transmission by fomites contaminated with vesicular secretions.
  • The varicella-zoster virus is highly contagious: it attacks 90% of contacts. susceptible.
  • The period of maximum contagiousness extends from 1-2 days before the rash until all the lesions are crusted (approximately 5-7 days after the onset of the rash).
  • In the hospital setting, contact and respiratory isolation is required until all the lesions are crusted.

items.foco: Most common complication: bacterial superinfection with S. aureus
items.title: Bacterial superinfection
items.contentHtml:
Superinfection by Staphylococcus aureus

  • The most common complication of chickenpox in children is bacterial superinfection of skin lesions, mainly by Staphylococcus aureus and, secondly, by Streptococcus pyogenes.
  • It presents as secondary impetigo, cellulitis, abscesses or necrotizing fasciitis.
  • Clinically, the vesicles become cloudy, with perilesional erythema, yellowish (meliceric) crusts and persistent or recurrent fever.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; Gram stain and culture of the lesion confirm the agent.
  • Empiric treatment should cover Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes (e.g. cephalexin, clindamycin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole depending on local resistance).

items.foco: Varicella pneumonia in adults and immunocompromised
items.title: varicella pneumonia
items.contentHtml:
Varicella-zoster virus pneumonia

  • Varicella-zoster pneumonia is the most serious visceral complication in adults and immunocompromised patients.
  • It appears 1-6 days after the rash, with cough, dyspnea, pleuritic pain and hemoptysis.
  • Chest x-ray: nodular infiltrates. bilateral (multiple nodules of 5-10 mm) that can coalesce, without hilar lymphadenopathy.
  • Risk factors: smoking, pregnancy (third trimester), immunosuppression and chronic lung disease.
  • Treatment requires intravenous acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 7-10 days and ventilatory support if necessary.

items.foco: Reye syndrome associated with aspirin in children
items.title: Reye's syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Reye's Syndrome

  • Reye's Syndrome is an acute encephalopathy with hepatic steatosis, associated with the use of aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) during viral infections such as chickenpox or influenza.
  • It occurs in children and adolescents with persistent vomiting, lethargy, irritability, seizures and coma.
  • Laboratory: elevated transaminases (aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase), elevated serum ammonium, prolonged prothrombin time, without significant hyperbilirubinemia.
  • Liver biopsy: fatty microvesicles in hepatocytes (microvesicular steatosis).
  • Absolute contraindication to the use of aspirin in children under 18 years of age with fever due to chickenpox or influenza; paracetamol or ibuprofen is preferred.

items.foco: Severe neonatal chickenpox if mother infected 5 days before delivery
items.title: Neonatal chicken pox
items.contentHtml:
Severe neonatal chickenpox

  • Severe neonatal chickenpox occurs when the mother develops the rash between 5 days before delivery and 2 days after delivery.
  • In this scenario, the newborn does not receive protective maternal antibodies (insufficient humoral immunity) and the infection is disseminated.
  • It manifests in the first 5-10 days of life with extensive skin lesions, pneumonia, hepatitis and encephalitis; mortality without treatment up to 30%.
  • Treatment includes intravenous acyclovir (20 mg/kg every 8 hours) plus specific immunoglobulin against varicella-zoster virus (VZIG) 125 units intramuscularly, ideally within 96 hours post-exposure.
  • Prophylaxis with VZIG is indicated in all newborns born to mothers with chickenpox. perinatal.

items.foco: Zoster due to reactivation of VZV in dorsal ganglia
items.title: Shingles
items.contentHtml:
Zoster (reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus)

  • Herpes zoster is the reactivation of the latent varicella-zoster virus in the dorsal root ganglia (sensory).
  • It presents as a unilateral, painful vesicular rash, with a dermatomal distribution (most common thoracic, then lumbar, cervical and trigeminal).
  • Neuropathic pain (postherpetic neuralgia) can persist for months; the risk increases with age (>50 years) and immunosuppression.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; in doubtful cases, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) of the vesicular fluid can be performed.
  • Treatment: acyclovir oral 800 mg 5 times a day, valacyclovir 1 g every 8 hours or famciclovir 500 mg every 8 hours for 7 days; start within the first 72 hours of the rash.

items.foco: Clinical diagnosis; PCR or immunofluorescence in doubtful cases
items.title: Diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis of chickenpox

  • The diagnosis is clinical in most cases: centripetal rash with lesions in different stages and a history of exposure.
  • In doubtful cases (immunocompromised, atypical lesions, differential diagnosis with disseminated herpes simplex or eczema herpeticum), it is confirmed with polymerase chain reaction (PCR) of the vesicular fluid, which is the gold standard.
  • Direct immunofluorescence of cells from scrapings from the base of the vesicle is another rapid and specific option.
  • Serology (immunoglobulin M or G) can be useful to determine susceptibility status or recent infection, but is not first line for acute diagnosis.
  • Viral culture is not recommended due to its low sensitivity and slowness.

items.foco: Contact and respiratory isolation until all lesions are scabbed
items.title: Isolation
items.contentHtml:
Hospital isolation

  • Patients with chickenpox require contact and respiratory isolation (standard precautions plus drops and contact) until all lesions are crusted.
  • In the hospital setting, the patient should be placed in a single room with negative pressure if possible.
  • Susceptible healthcare personnel (without a history of chickenpox or vaccination) should avoid exposure; if it occurs, post-exposure prophylaxis is evaluated.
  • The duration of isolation is approximately 5-7 days from the onset of the rash, but is prolonged if new lesions appear.
  • In immunocompromised patients, dissemination may be longer; Isolation is maintained until all lesions are dry and crusted.

items.foco: Oral acyclovir in high-risk adults and adolescents
items.title: Antiviral treatment
items.contentHtml:
Acyclovir in chickenpox

  • Antiviral treatment is indicated in adults (over 18 years of age), adolescents over 12 years of age, patients with chronic disease (skin, lung, kidney) and in treatment with systemic or inhaled corticosteroids at high doses.
  • The drug of choice is oral acyclovir at a dose of 20 mg/kg/dose (maximum 800 mg) 5 times a day for 7 days, started within the first 24-48 hours of the rash.
  • In healthy children under 12 years of age it is not routinely recommended, unless they have risk factors or moderate-severe disease.
  • In immunocompromised patients or with complications (pneumonia, encephalitis), intravenous acyclovir is administered 10 mg/kg every 8 hours.
  • Alternatives: oral valacyclovir (not approved for children under 2 years of age) and oral famciclovir (over 18 years of age).

items.foco: Live attenuated virus vaccine; contraindicated in pregnancy and immunosuppressed
items.title: Vaccination
items.contentHtml:
Vaccine against chickenpox

  • The vaccine contains live attenuated virus (Oka/Merck strain). It is administered subcutaneously in two doses: first at 12-15 months and second at 4-6 years.
  • Absolute contraindications: pregnancy (theoretical risk of congenital malformations), severe cellular immunodeficiency (leukemia, lymphoma, human immunodeficiency virus infection with CD4+ T lymphocyte count <200 cells/μL), anaphylactic allergy to any component (neomycin, gelatin).
  • In patients with mild immunosuppression (e.g. low-dose corticosteroids) it can be considered under specialized evaluation.
  • The vaccine is effective in preventing chickenpox (>90% for moderate-severe disease) and also reduces the risk of herpes zoster (non-latent vaccine strain).
  • In contact with a case of chickenpox, the post-exposure vaccine (within 3-5 days) can prevent or attenuate the disease.
